items.title: An Append-Only Record Needs A Physical Append Operation, Not An Insertion Anchored To Text
items.problem: Session 023's two continuity entries were committed into the middle of the old monolithic Batch 003.md — inside Session 021's closed record — instead of appended after the actual most recent session. The edit was anchored to the first '<!-- [VXG RealForever] -->' marker in the file, treating the continuity marker like an end-of-file sentinel in a code file. The record stayed structurally valid markdown, so nothing failed loudly; the damage was semantic: the two newest sessions became invisible to the documented cold-start path ('scroll to the bottom, read the most recent session'), were mis-attributed to an older session by a different instance, and carried none of the required session metadata (date, instance, thread).
items.lesson: A convention that says 'append only' but is implemented as 'edit a shared file at the right spot' will eventually be violated by any writer that anchors its edit on searchable text — the same hazard class as sentinel-based tools corrupting themselves. When a record must be append-only, choose a storage shape where appending is the only physically natural operation (new file in a directory beats new block in a file) and where the ordering key lives in metadata the file system already sorts (a date-prefixed filename), not in position within a document.
items.impact: The misplacement failure mode is structurally impossible for new sessions: closed sessions are closed files. Session 023 was relocated with corrected attribution and a provenance note rather than silently rewritten. Regression-tested in tests/14-session-bootstrap.test.js (latestSessionFile unit tests plus an integration test binding the registry row to a real directory and a real newest session file).

items.title: "Confirmed Correct on the Server" Is Not "Confirmed Visible to the User" — Every Caching Layer Between Them Can Independently Hide a Real Fix
items.problem: Victor reported Chinese missing from the language FAB on the dossier page. Across one continuous session, four independent root causes were found and fixed, each confirmed correct on GitHub's main branch at the time — and the symptom persisted through all but the last: (1) widgets/fab-lang.js never read its own build-time-baked window.VEX_SUPPORTED_LANGS global, falling through to a stale localStorage cache or network fetch instead (PR #79); (2) that network fetch's own cache-busting VERSION constant had never been bumped since introduction, so jsdelivr's CDN kept serving a stale index.json indefinitely regardless of what changed on main (PR #82, generalized into an auto-incrementing scheme in PR #83); (3) once the dossier was wired to load its God Script directly (bypassing jsdelivr for that page entirely, PR #84), a newly-activated Service Worker could install a new version but never signaled the already-open tab to use it (PR #86); (4) even with that signal wired up, the Service Worker's own background update CHECK is throttled by the browser to roughly once per 24 hours, so reloading the page — even a hard reload — did not reliably trigger a fresh check at all, meaning the fix from (3) had nothing to react to (PR #87, confirmed only after Victor pushed back and asked for an actual reproduction instead of another assumption-based patch). A separate instance (Codex, PR #89) later found two more root causes this session's own investigation never reached because it scoped to one page: (5) the localStorage cache in widgets/fab-lang.js's STANDALONE fallback path (any page still loading the widget directly from the CDN instead of a God Script) short-circuited the network fetch entirely on a cache hit — meaning any such page could freeze on a stale language list forever, independent of every fix above, since none of them touch the standalone path; (6) pages/specimen-architectural-wisdoms.html had a hand-authored `window.VEX_SUPPORTED_LANGS = ['en', 'ja']` inline script — because PR #79's own fix makes that exact global win first, this hardcoded fake permanently shadowed every other fix in the chain for that one page specifically.
items.lesson: A codebase with N independent caching layers between 'the fix shipped' and 'the user sees it' can produce up to N rounds of 'still not visible' before the actual last layer is found — and each round can look, in isolation, like a fully-diagnosed, fully-verified fix, because each one WAS correct for the layer it addressed. The tell that you're mid-stack rather than done is the symptom recurring after a fix that was itself correctly reasoned and server-verified. When that happens, the right move is not a fifth theory — it's reproducing the actual user-visible lifecycle (a simulation, a real device, a real reload cycle) to find out definitively whether you're looking at a new layer or an incomplete fix to the one you thought you'd closed. Server-side correctness and client-visible correctness are different claims requiring different evidence, and caching is exactly where the two can silently diverge for arbitrarily long. A second, related tell surfaced only after this lesson was first written: fixing the bug on the one page it was reported on can leave the same bug fully intact on every other page that shares the vulnerable code path, especially when the fix works by moving the reported page OFF that path rather than fixing the path itself — always ask 'does this fix the pattern, or just this instance,' and check every other consumer of the same widget/global/contract before calling the investigation closed.
items.impact: Five merged/pending PRs (#79, #82/#83, #84, #86/#87, #89), all labeled bug for retraceability. No single code change generalizes this — it's a standing verification discipline for any future change that touches a browser-cacheable asset in this repo, named here so the next instance recognizes the pattern on fix #2 instead of shipping fix #4 believing each one alone was the answer, and checks every other consumer of a shared widget/global before declaring a fix complete.

items.title: A Green CI Run Only Proves the Environments CI Actually Exercises
items.problem: After Session 024 reported 345/345 tests passing on GitHub/Linux, a Windows checkout with Git core.autocrlf=true failed the same health path: node lib/build-lattice-headers.js --check reported all 31 covered JS files as drifted, which made lib/session-bootstrap.js and the test suite fail locally. The lattice header content was semantically identical; the generator had hardcoded LF line endings while the working tree files were CRLF. GitHub Actions never saw the failure because it ran on ubuntu-latest with LF files, and the existing idempotence tests only used LF string fixtures.
items.lesson: When a check compares bytes, paths, file modes, line endings, clocks, shell behavior, device APIs, or runtime capabilities, do not treat one green CI platform as proof of general correctness. Name the environment assumption, then either make the code representation-aware (preserve the file's own convention, query the runtime capability, use the platform-neutral API) or add a health check that exercises the other real environment. This same discipline will matter more in Android/mobile work, where stability means a mapped set of devices, OS versions, permissions, storage states, and lifecycle transitions, not 'it passed once on the emulator I happened to use.'
items.impact: Local Windows tests now pass without changing 31 unrelated lattice headers; node lib/build-lattice-headers.js --check reports zero drift on CRLF working-tree files; the full suite reports 346/346 passing. The broader practice was added to docs/culture.md and docs/architecture/11-debugging-practices.md.

items.title: A Generated File's Metadata Must Not Change Unless Its Real Content Does — Otherwise Every Independent Rebuild Is a Manufactured Merge Conflict
items.problem: PR #50 hit a real GitHub merge conflict in data/status.json: the branch had regenerated the file after PR #49 merged, main's own auto-rebuild commit (.github/workflows/build-index.yml) had also regenerated it independently in the meantime, and the two copies conflicted — not because the underlying tech-debt/enhancement/open-discussion content actually differed, but because lib/build-status.js stamped _meta.generated (a wall-clock ISO timestamp) and _meta.commit (the git SHA at build time) into the file on every single run. Two builds of identical source data produced two different files purely because of when and on what commit each one happened to run.
items.lesson: Before stamping a wall-clock timestamp or a commit SHA into any file this build pipeline regenerates and commits, ask who actually reads that field. If the answer is nobody, it is not metadata — it is a guaranteed source of spurious merge conflicts on every PR that happens to overlap an unrelated rebuild, and the fix is to remove it, not to reach for a smarter conflict-resolution mechanism (merge drivers, custom scripts) to paper over a self-inflicted problem. Content-determinism (same input → byte-identical output) should be the default assumption for any generated artifact; a field that breaks it needs to earn its place by being genuinely consumed (data/index.json's builtAt, sw.js's cache-bust name), not just be conventional decoration.
items.impact: Removed the field entirely from data/status.json's schema (tests/10-build-status.test.js updated to assert its absence and to assert byte-identical output for identical input). Directly closes the class of merge conflict that blocked PR #50. No other generated artifact in this pipeline was found to have the same unused-volatility problem (checked data/index.json's builtAt and sw.js's cache name — both are legitimately consumed and were left alone).

items.title: A .gitattributes merge=ours Declaration Does Nothing Without a Locally-Registered Driver
items.problem: docs/architecture.md's constraint #8 documents that generated files (data/index.json, pages/archives.html, docs/architecture.md, etc.) use merge=ours in .gitattributes so a feature-branch rebase automatically keeps main's built version instead of producing a conflict. Rebasing this branch onto main after a bot auto-rebuild commit produced a real CONFLICT in data/index.json anyway — the attribute was present, but nothing resolved automatically.
items.lesson: merge=ours in .gitattributes is not self-executing. It only takes effect in an environment where the merge driver has been separately registered in git config — a step that lives outside the repository and does not survive a fresh clone or a new session/container. Any environment that clones this repo fresh (including this one) should expect generated-file conflicts to surface as real conflicts, not to be silently resolved, and must fall back to the manual take-theirs(main)-then-rebuild procedure every time, not just when something seems to have gone wrong.
items.impact: No code change — this is a documented expectation for future sessions rather than a fix, since the actual gap (driver registration) lives in git config the repo can't carry. Worth adding a line to docs/architecture.md's constraint #8 if a future session wants to make this explicit rather than rediscover it.

items.title: From Hunting to Blueprint
items.problem: Adding a feature required reading 5+ files to infer the pattern. Each file only showed part of the contract. Errors were likely.
items.lesson: The blueprint is the map. The code is the territory. Read the map first.
items.impact: 5-minute feature addition instead of 30 minutes of inference. Self-documenting system.

items.title: Two Independently-Authored Documents Converged on This Repo's Own Discipline — Worth Recording Explicitly, Not Filed as Coincidence
items.problem: Victor shared four external design-document URLs (bridge-council, bridge-council-os, bridge-council-schema, org-blueprint) and asked whether a single AI instance could hold 'multi-lens' council awareness internally instead of coordinating across multiple separate instances. Two of the referenced documents (org-blueprint.html, org-history.html) were already sitting in this repo's pages/ directory, uncurated, named in every check-key-alignment report all session, but never actually read for what they described until this pass.
items.lesson: When external material independently reflects a principle already practiced here, say so specifically (what converged, and why it's not coincidental) rather than silently absorbing it or treating it as unrelated background reading. Naming a convergence is itself evidence-gathering: it tells a future reader that a principle has now been independently validated from two directions, which is a stronger claim than either direction supports alone — and that claim is only available to a future reader if someone writes it down.
items.impact: docs/architecture/14-council-model.md and data/council-kernel.json now exist; org-blueprint.html, org-history.html, bridge-council.html, witness-committee-operations.html, and human-ai-corelational-governance.html are placed under a new institute department (org-design/governance workTypes) via config/content-intents.json rather than hand-edited. No claim is made that the 'multi-lens single instance' idea is fully designed or adopted as standing practice — see the architecture doc's honest-limits section for what's proposed versus what's real.

items.title: JSON Keys Are Strings — The Mitigation Is Validation
items.problem: Named constants (Category.DEMO, Feature.LANG) work in JavaScript but not in JSON. Slug keys in index.json are raw strings — they can drift from nodes.json without a type error.
items.lesson: JSON is string-based. Keys are strings. This is unavoidable. The mitigation is validation, not elimination.
items.impact: Key drift caught on every PR via GitHub Actions comment. No false-blocking CI gate.

items.title: From Magic Strings to Named Constants
items.problem: Hardcoded strings scattered in build scripts — 'demo', 'ja', 'production'. IDE can't navigate to their meaning. Typos fail silently.
items.lesson: The variable name is the intention. The string value is irrelevant.
items.impact: IDE-navigable, self-documenting, auditable via grep for constant usage.

items.title: From Multiple Files to God Script
items.problem: 5-7 requests per page — shell.js, vextreme.js, lang-fab.js, demo-fab.js, scope bundles. Each is a fetch and a potential failure point.
items.lesson: The build script is the assembler. The browser receives the output.
items.impact: Reduced requests by 80%, eliminated runtime failure points for EN strings.

items.title: A Test Protocol and Its Test Log Are Not the Same Document, Even When They're the Same File
items.problem: pages/witness-committee-operations.html mixes two different kinds of content in one hand-authored HTML file: sections 00–04 and 06 are a forward-facing test protocol (opening question, per-document verification questions, the test sequence, a blank checklist template) that describes how to run a future Witness Committee test, while section 05 (Test Record Index) is a historical log of tests already run (TEST 01, TEST 01-B, TEST 02 pending). The file was built once, in one pass, with no structural line between 'the procedure for next time' and 'what happened last time' — because at the time it was written there was only ever going to be one test, then two. Claude raised a concern about the document's framing without first being clear about which part it meant, and Victor pushed back hard, correctly reading the initial framing as risking the same evidence-free pattern-matching mistake made earlier this session on wip/silent-god.json (see the convos_with_god / declarative-content lesson already in culture.md).
items.lesson: When a single artifact contains both 'the rule for doing X' and 'a record of X having been done,' those two halves have different mutability rules even if they live in the same file: the record is closed the moment the event it describes is over (editing it after the fact is falsifying evidence, not correcting a draft), while the rule stays open to revision for as long as it hasn't yet produced the next recorded event. Before raising any concern about a document like this, identify which half is actually being questioned — 'this test's logged result seems off' and 'the next test's procedure could be run differently' are different claims requiring different justification, and confusing them (as happened here on the first pass) reads as exactly the same evidence-free judgment Victor already corrected once this session.
items.impact: No code or content change. Establishes the standing distinction for any future test/record document in this repo, and flags pages/witness-committee-operations.html as a candidate (not yet approved) for splitting into a revisable protocol/template part and a separate append-only test-log part, since it was authored before this repo had that convention.

items.title: A Sentinel-Based Tool Must Treat Mentions Of Its Own Sentinel As Hazardous
items.problem: Three separate bugs, same root cause: a tool that searches text for a marker or delimiter (JSDoc's */, this repo's LATTICE:BEGIN/END, a literal /** substring) corrupted a file the moment that exact text appeared in prose describing the tool itself, not in the tool's real output. lib/strings-compile.js's glob pattern closed a JSDoc block early. lib/build-lattice-headers.js's own lattice-map.json entry, describing what it writes, confused its own marker search. lib/logger-codes.js had a comment naming this exact hazard class, containing the literal substring '/**', which a bare indexOf('/**') mistook for a real doc comment opener.
items.lesson: A sentinel-based tool's search has no concept of 'inside vs. outside a comment describing the tool' — it is a plain substring match. Any prose the tool itself writes, or any file it processes, that happens to mention the sentinel is a hazard exactly as real as a genuine one. Defend against this before shipping a sentinel-based tool, not after the third occurrence.
items.impact: Two general-purpose defenses (sanitizeForComment, findLineStartDocComment) now cover every instance of this hazard class found so far, rather than three independent patches for three independent symptoms. Regression-tested in tests/11-lattice-headers.test.js.

items.title: Token Cost Tracks What Comes Back Into Context, Not Where Code Ran — and Judgment Can't Be Delegated to a Function
items.problem: Victor asked whether pushing a declared intent through GitHub Actions and waiting for a webhook notification would save Claude tokens compared to verifying locally before committing, on the assumption that GitHub Actions' compute is free for Claude while local tool calls burn tokens. A related, separate question followed in the same exchange: whether Claude should rely on more pre-built 'blueprint' functions instead of 'recalculating with assumptions', framed as a related possible token-cost optimization.
items.lesson: When asked whether an operation could be made cheaper, first ask whether the cost is coming from the mechanics (which a deterministic function already handles or could handle) or from the judgment (which cannot be delegated to a function no matter how it's built). Only the mechanical half is genuinely optimizable via better functions or a 'blueprint' library; conflating the two risks either overengineering functions that can't do the actual job, or wrongly assuming a judgment step could be skipped for a token discount. A future instance asked a similar cost-optimization question should separate 'is this mechanical or judgment' before proposing an architecture change.
items.impact: No code change. Standing clarification for future sessions on where token-cost optimization genuinely applies in this repo's tooling (mechanical execution — already handled by lib/*.js's pure functions and the content-intents pipeline) versus where a semantic judgment step is unavoidable and shouldn't be assumed away.

items.title: data/arcs.json (v1) and data/arcs-v2.json (v2) Share No Runtime Path — v1 Is Frozen, Not a File to Keep in Sync
items.problem: Before building any arc-membership automation for the v2 pipeline (a vex:arcs meta-tag / build-time slug-insertion feature, requested Session 022), Victor raised a real, previously-unresolved concern: does data/arcs.json (which the still-live Squarespace site fetches at runtime) depend on or get affected by anything done to data/arcs-v2.json (the v2 GitHub Pages build pipeline's source)? Neither party had full recall of whether that dependency existed.
items.lesson: Two files that look like versioned copies of the same concept (same arc keys, same apparent purpose) can share zero runtime path — confirm the actual consumer set before assuming a dependency exists in either direction. The risk here wasn't 'v2 changes could break v1' (they can't — nothing during a v2 build ever touches data/arcs.json) or 'v1 changes could break v2' (same reason) — the real, subtler risk was that new arc-insertion tooling built for v2 only would silently drift from v1's current parity going forward, if v1 were still an active target. Once confirmed it isn't, that risk doesn't apply, but it would apply again the moment either file resumes taking new content — worth re-checking if that ever changes.
items.impact: No code behavior change. Fixed one stale/incorrect code comment (lib/build-archives.js's ARC_KEY_MAP). Documented the full v1/v2 relationship in docs/architecture/03-data.md so it doesn't need re-tracing. Unblocks building vex:arcs-style arc-insertion automation against data/arcs-v2.json alone, without needing to also write data/arcs.json or build a parity check for it.
